summary: The paper targets a gap in how LLMs are evaluated for operations research: most existing benchmarks treat OR as a one-shot translation from a natural-language description to solver code and ignore the iterative diagnostic loop that practitioners actually use to repair infeasible models. The authors formalize infeasible-model repair as a solver-in-the-loop Markov Decision Process, in which each repair action triggers solver re-execution and recomputation of the Irreducible Infeasible Subsystem (IIS), yielding deterministic, verifiable feedback. They introduce ORLoopBench, which has two parts: ORDebug, a suite of 5,362 LP/MILP infeasible-model repair instances spanning nine error types, and ORBias, an evaluation of operational decision rationality in inventory/newsvendor settings against closed-form optimal policies. On top of the benchmark the authors build a domain-specific training pipeline for an 8B model (SFT, GRPO with composite process rewards, an optional process reward model, and curriculum learning) and report that solver-verified RLVR training lets the 8B model surpass frontier APIs on LP repair (95.3% vs 92.4% RR@5), improves diagnostic behavior, and transfers to MILP repair. They further show that whole-model code regeneration can yield feasible but semantically drifted MILPs that solve the wrong problem, motivating process-level evaluation with solver oracles.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper addresses a meaningful and well-motivated problem. Framing OR evaluation as iterative, solver-in-the-loop self-correction is a better match to real practitioner workflows than one-shot translation, and the diagnostic loop built on deterministic IIS feedback is a sensible way to obtain verifiable process-level signals. ORDebug is substantial in scale (over 5,000 instances covering nine error types) and its construction is thoughtful: controlled sabotage to induce infeasibility, oracle/solver verification, and evaluation metrics that go beyond raw success to include diagnostic accuracy. The empirical study is broad and reasonably careful, spanning 26 local and API models with per-error-type breakdowns, inference-scaling analysis, and ablations over the training components (PRM, RLVR/GRPO, curriculum). The resulting training recipe offers actionable guidance and a clear headline result, with an 8B model surpassing much larger frontier systems on LP repair and transferring to MILP. The observation that feasible regenerated MILPs can silently solve the wrong problem is a nice illustration of why process-level, solver-grounded evaluation matters.

The main reservations concern scope and completeness rather than experimental execution. Coverage is limited: ORDebug centers on infeasibility repair and is primarily LP-based, whereas LLM-generated OR failures also include code/execution errors and feasible-but-suboptimal solutions, which may be at least as common in practice; ORBias, meanwhile, is confined to newsvendor settings. The representativeness of these choices for the broader space of OR tasks is asserted more than demonstrated. The two components also sit somewhat unevenly under the solver-in-the-loop banner, since ORBias does not actually use solver feedback or iterative repair, weakening its connection to the paper's central framing. Comparisons with existing OR-domain models and self-correction systems are missing; without evaluating prior approaches such as ORLM, solver-informed RL, multi-round self-correction pipelines, or agentic debugging methods under the same solver-feedback setting, it is hard to establish that solver-in-the-loop self-correction is a genuine unaddressed gap rather than simply a new evaluation setting. The end-to-end utility of the trained models is also not shown: they are not integrated into a full formulation-to-solution pipeline to measure downstream gains. In terms of contribution character, the work is closer to a strong benchmark-and-evaluation effort combined with a stack of established training techniques than to a new methodological advance, and because it brings together two benchmarks, several metrics, SFT/GRPO/PRM, and curriculum learning at once, the central takeaways are somewhat diffuse. Finally, the ablations reveal an unresolved tension between recovery rate (RR@5) and diagnostic accuracy: the best combined method does not achieve the best DA, so the "better diagnosis" narrative would benefit from clearer articulation. Presentation is understandable but could be improved: the manuscript is notation- and acronym-heavy, some core ideas need more intuitive exposition, and several figures are dense with small text and appear to be raster rather than vector graphics, which hurts clarity. On originality, the solver-in-the-loop MDP with IIS-based feedback is a reasonably fresh framing, though the training pipeline assembles known components and the error-injection style of data construction has precedents.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. How do existing OR-domain models and self-correction systems (e.g., ORLM, solver-informed RL, multi-round self-correction pipelines such as LLMOPT, and agentic debugging approaches such as OptiMUS) perform under the solver-feedback setting of ORDebug? This would help establish that solver-in-the-loop self-correction is a genuine gap rather than only a new setting.
2. How should readers interpret the trade-off between recovery rate and diagnostic accuracy, given that PRM most improves DA while curriculum+GRPO yields the best RR@5 but lower DA?
3. Why is ORBias restricted to newsvendor scenarios, and how representative are ORDebug's LP-centric infeasibility instances of the broader space of OR failures, which also includes code/execution errors and feasible-but-suboptimal solutions?
4. How sensitive are the ORDebug results to random seed and decoding settings?
5. Can the trained repair and bias-mitigation models be integrated into an end-to-end formulation-to-solution pipeline to demonstrate improvements on the full task rather than only on the isolated repair step?
limitations: The limitations discussion is underdeveloped and should be expanded. The authors should explicitly acknowledge the restricted scope (infeasibility-focused debugging that is primarily LP-based, and behavioral evaluation confined to newsvendor settings), the absence of end-to-end pipeline validation, and the fact that the training approach combines established techniques rather than introducing a new method.
